plans.subject: social_studies
plans.default_goal: Students understand what Matariki is and why it matters.
plans.routing_reason: Matariki is currently configured as a photo-led cultural/factual pack with explicit local-context review.
sources: Te Papa: What and who is Matariki?
Te Papa: The stars of Matariki
NZ Government: Public holidays and anniversary dates
NASA: Interstellar Cloud in the Pleiades
worksheet_questions: What is Matariki?
The Maori name for the Pleiades star cluster and a time associated with the Maori New Year.
Name one principle of Matariki.
Remembrance, celebration, or looking to the future.
Why should local context be checked?
Traditions vary by iwi and region.
Write one respectful question you still have.
Answers vary.
review_notes: Local iwi context should be checked before classroom use.
Do not present one model as universal.

plans.subject: science
plans.default_goal: Students understand how the Solar System is organised around the Sun.
plans.routing_reason: Solar System is configured as a photo-led factual science pack using trusted public space imagery.
sources: NASA Science: Solar System Family Portrait
worksheet_questions: What is at the centre of the Solar System?
The Sun.
How many major planets orbit the Sun?
Eight.
Why are classroom Solar System diagrams often not to scale?
The distances are too large compared with the planet sizes.
Name one smaller object type in the Solar System.
Moons, asteroids, comets, dwarf planets, or smaller space material.
review_notes: Explain when a visual is a model or composite.
Distances and sizes are simplified for Year 5.

plans.subject: science
plans.default_goal: Students understand the core ideas of Volcanoes.
plans.routing_reason: Volcanoes is configured as a labelled cross-section topic, so the diagram-led lane is preferred.
sources: USGS: Understanding volcanic hazards can save lives
USGS: Comprehensive monitoring provides timely warnings of volcano reawakening
worksheet_questions: What is magma?
Molten rock below Earth's surface.
What is lava?
Magma that has reached Earth's surface.
Which volcano part is the main pathway upward?
The main vent.
Name one possible volcanic hazard.
Lava flows, ash, gases, lahars, or pyroclastic flows.
review_notes: This is a simplified Year 4 model of a volcano cross-section.
Teacher should review local hazard examples before discussing local risk.
Diagram visuals are deterministic SVG, not AI-generated factual imagery.

plans.subject: science
plans.default_goal: Students identify Earth's main layers and explain that diagrams are simplified models.
plans.routing_reason: Earth's Layers is a labelled cross-section topic with clear parts, so the diagram-led lane is preferred.
sources: USGS: The Interior of the Earth
NASA Space Place: What Is an Earthquake?
worksheet_questions: Which layer is the thin outside layer of Earth?
The crust.
Which layer sits below the crust?
The mantle.
Which core layer is liquid?
The outer core.
Why is an Earth's layers diagram a model?
It simplifies Earth's inside so people can understand the main layers.
review_notes: This is a simplified Year 5 model of Earth's layers.
Layer thickness and shape are adjusted for classroom visibility.
Diagram visuals are deterministic SVG, not copied from the TeacherGPT reference PDFs.

plans.subject: mathematics
plans.default_goal: Students practise representing, comparing, and explaining simple fractions.
plans.routing_reason: Fractions is currently configured as a print-first mathematics practice pack, so worksheet and homework lanes are preferred.
worksheet_questions: Write the shaded amount as a fraction: 3 parts shaded out of 8 equal parts.
Circle the larger fraction: 1/2 or 1/4.
Write one fraction equivalent to 1/2.
2/4, 3/6, 4/8, or another correct equivalent fraction.
A pizza is cut into 6 equal slices. Sam eats 2 slices. What fraction did Sam eat?
2/6, or 1/3 if simplified.
Explain what the denominator tells you.
It tells how many equal parts make the whole.
Explain what the numerator tells you.
It tells how many equal parts are counted or shaded.
review_notes: This is standard arithmetic practice and does not require factual source grounding.
Teacher should check notation and difficulty against the class programme.
No external image assets are required.

plans.subject: science
plans.default_goal: Students explain that tsunamis are a series of waves caused by sudden movement of water, often after seafloor movement.
plans.routing_reason: Tsunamis is configured as a deterministic process diagram plus reading-response worksheet using NOAA source grounding.
sources: NOAA Ocean Service: What is a tsunami?
worksheet_questions: What is a tsunami?
A series of ocean waves caused by sudden movement of water.
What can move suddenly before many tsunamis begin?
The seafloor, often because of an undersea earthquake.
Why can tsunami waves grow near land?
They slow in shallow water and can grow taller.
Is a tsunami one wave or a series of waves?
A series of waves.
Name one possible tsunami trigger.
Undersea earthquake, landslide, volcanic activity, or another sudden water displacement.
Why should local safety advice be checked?
Local hazards and emergency guidance depend on the place and official advice.
review_notes: This is a simplified classroom model of tsunami formation.
Use local civil defence or emergency guidance when discussing real local safety actions.
Diagram visuals are deterministic SVG, not AI-generated factual imagery.
